turns.text: Hello Elna, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s a bit strange, sitting here in this plastic chair, trying to put words to something that feels so raw and constant. Honestly, I’m tired. Between the extra weight and the constant mental math of trying to stretch a low income across everything this baby will need, I feel like I’m constantly holding my breath.

I’m grateful, truly—I know this is a "low-risk" pregnancy, and in some circles, that’s supposed to mean I should just be happy everything is standard. But "standard" is exactly what scares me. With my first, I felt like a passenger in my own body. Everything was so fast, so clinical. There was no room for me to breathe or think, just people shouting protocols at me while I was trying to figure out how to be a mother for the first time. 

Now, being here in Dudley, it feels like I’m walking into a machine. I know the history of this—I’ve read how we lost the village midwives, how birth became this thing that’s "managed" by men in white coats rather than experienced by women in their own homes. I don’t want to be a chart number or a time slot on someone’s lunch break. I want to feel human, but it’s hard when the system is built to make you feel like a product on an assembly line. I’m just trying to figure out how to keep my agency in a room that feels designed to take it away.
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

It was early, just as the sun was starting to bleed through the thin curtains of my apartment in Dudley. The room felt quiet, almost too still, like the air itself was holding its breath. I was sitting on the edge of the tub, the cold tile biting into my thighs, staring at that little plastic stick like it was an alien artifact.

I was alone. The house was empty, just the hum of the refrigerator in the other room. My first thought wasn't joy—it was a sharp, jabbing panic about the logistics. I started counting months in my head, calculating how my job would hold up and how the money would stretch—or not stretch. It felt like I was staring at a cliff edge.

I didn't call anyone immediately. I just sat there, tracing the lines of the floorboards with my eyes, feeling this overwhelming sense of—what do you call it? A weight, but a heavy, ancestral kind of weight. I thought about the cycle of it all, how my mother and her mother did this, and how I was about to walk into that same medical system that seems to view us more as biology than as people. I tucked the test back into its box, hid it in the bottom of my bag, and went to make a cup of tea. I needed the heat to steady my hands. Everything changed in that heartbeat, but the world outside didn't care. It just kept turning.
When you decided who to tell first — or whether to tell anyone at all — what was behind that decision? What felt safe or risky about sharing the news?

That’s the thing, isn’t it? When you don't have a safety net—when every dollar is already spoken for—telling people feels like opening the gates to a storm of opinions you can't afford to deal with.

I didn't tell my family right away. With my first, there was so much "advice" that sounded a lot like judgment. People look at you differently when you’re unmarried and struggling. They stop asking "How are you?" and start asking "How are you going to manage?" as if my poverty somehow disqualifies me from the joy of bringing a life into the world. It’s patronizing, and it makes you feel like you’re doing something wrong just by existing.

I kept it quiet for a few weeks because I needed to reclaim the space for myself first. I wanted to just be Elna, the woman who was going to be a mother again, before I became "the girl who’s having another one with nothing to her name." It felt risky to share because I knew the looks I’d get. Sharing the news felt like handing over a piece of my autonomy, inviting the world to scrutinize my finances and my choices. I wanted to hold onto the strength of it—my strength—for as long as I could before it became public property, or worse, a medical record.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

The air in the room just… stopped. One minute I was watching the grainy, flickering screen, trying to spot a heartbeat, and the next, the room felt like a vacuum. That silence hit me like a physical blow. 

My first instinct wasn't even about the baby—it was about the power dynamic. I immediately felt that familiar, cold sweat of being a "case." My mind raced: *Is it something I did? Is it my diet? Is the system going to start taking over now?* I felt small, like I was shrinking into the paper gown. I knew that if there was a problem, the medical staff would move to "fix" it, likely without asking me what I wanted or how I felt. They’d speak over me, to each other, using words that feel like barricades.

I didn't say much at first. My throat was tight, and my hands were white-knuckled on the sides of the exam table. I think I whispered, "Is everything okay?" but my voice sounded like it belonged to someone else. I felt this intense, desperate need to protect the sanctity of the moment, even if it was turning into a medical crisis. I kept repeating to myself, *I am still the mother. I am still the mother.* I just wanted them to look at me, not just the monitor. I wanted to be treated like a person whose world was potentially crumbling, not just a malfunctioning machine that needed troubleshooting.
In that moment, how much did you feel like you could ask questions or speak up — or did something stop you? What shaped that?

The silence from the sonographer felt like a wall. When you’re in that position—lying there, vulnerable, exposed, and honestly, a bit intimidated by the sheer amount of equipment surrounding you—it feels incredibly risky to speak up. I felt like if I asked the "wrong" question, I’d be labeled as the "difficult" patient, the one who’s questioning the experts. 

There’s this unspoken pressure to be the "good girl," to just nod and accept whatever they tell you. That’s the legacy of the system, isn't it? We’re taught that the person in the white coat holds all the cards, and the patient is just a recipient of information, not a participant in a conversation. Every time I thought about asking, "What exactly are you looking at?" or "Why are you staying on that spot so long?", I felt my own insecurities bubbling up. I worried that my low-income status or my unmarried state might make them see me as less capable of understanding or less worthy of a full, honest explanation. 

I was paralyzed by the fear that if I pushed too hard, they’d shut me out even further, making me a footnote in their report instead of a mother asking about her child. It’s that deep, hollow feeling that my voice simply doesn’t carry enough weight in that room.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

The way she presented it felt almost… scripted. It was a rapid-fire explanation of probabilities and "risks," delivered with that detached efficiency that makes you feel like you’re in a transaction, not a conversation about your child’s life. She made it sound so routine, like choosing a brand of cereal, but in my gut, it felt like an invitation to enter a labyrinth of anxiety I wasn't sure I could navigate.

I didn't answer immediately. I looked at her, really looked at her, searching for a sign that she actually saw me, but she was already half-turned toward her computer screen. That’s what decided it for me. I realized that if I said "yes," I was opting into a system that loves data points but rarely has time for the emotional fallout of what those points might reveal. I wasn't ready to have a stranger tell me, based on a percentage, how I should feel about my own pregnancy.

I told her I needed to think about it—though truthfully, I think I already knew. I want to keep this space as mine. If I’m going to be a mother, I want to meet my child on their own terms, not as a collection of screened outcomes. I don't need a statistic to tell me how to love this baby.
Did you feel you had enough time and information to make that decision freely — or did you sense any pressure, either to accept or decline?

Time is the one thing they never seem to have in those rooms. It’s always "tick-tock, we’re behind schedule," even though my appointment is the only thing defining my entire week. I felt a tremendous amount of subtle pressure to just say "yes" and get on with the checklist. When you’re low-income and in a public clinic, you get the distinct sense that you’re expected to be compliant. Asking for time or questioning the necessity of a test feels like you’re being a nuisance, like you’re wasting the "precious" time of the provider.

I didn't feel like I was making a choice freely; I felt like I was dodging a conveyor belt. The information they gave me was all about the clinical risk, never about the *human* experience of what happens if that risk turns into a reality. There was no space for me to say, "What would this mean for my life?" or "How do I process this alone?" 

It felt like they were pushing me to decide so they could check a box on their screen and move me to the next station. Declining felt like an act of rebellion, which is a terrifying thing to do when you’re literally lying under their lights. You’re never really "free" when you’re dependent on a system that views your hesitation as a hurdle to be cleared.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

It’s exhausting. It’s like trying to build a house on sand—every time I start to feel even a tiny bit comfortable, the ground shifts. Receiving news like that from a complete stranger makes the message feel colder, somehow. She doesn’t know my history, my fears, or how I’ve been struggling to keep my anxiety at bay. To her, I’m just a file that popped up on her screen. To me, this is my life, my body, and my baby.

When I have to repeat myself—explaining my situation over and over to someone who hasn’t even glanced at my notes—it just reinforces that feeling of being a "case." It makes me put my guard up instantly. If she doesn’t know me, how can I trust her to interpret these results in a way that’s actually helpful for *me*? I find myself holding back, barely asking questions, because I’m so busy trying to figure out if she’s someone I can even talk to. 

It makes the information feel disconnected from my reality. It’s just "data" being relayed by a voice on the other end of the line. I want someone to look me in the eye and hold the weight of this with me, but instead, I’m just another call on a busy afternoon. It’s isolating. It makes me feel like I’m navigating this whole thing in the dark, all by myself.
Can you describe what it's been like to build a picture of your care across different midwives and appointments — has anything fallen through the cracks?

It’s like trying to put together a puzzle where half the pieces are missing and the other half belong to a completely different picture. Every time I walk into that clinic, I feel like I’m starting from scratch. I’m the one who has to carry the burden of continuity, not them. I’m the one keeping track of my own symptoms, my own fears, and the tiny details that seem to slip through the cracks of their standardized forms.

Things absolutely fall through the cracks. It’s the little things, mostly—the way I mentioned being overwhelmed at my last visit, or the specific way I’ve been feeling about the birth plan. It’s never in the notes, or if it is, the next person hasn't bothered to read them. I spent twenty minutes once explaining a recurring pain that the previous midwife had brushed off, only to have the new one look at me with total confusion. 

I’m the only constant in this entire experience. The midwives are just visitors in my pregnancy. Because there’s no real relationship, there’s no trust. When you don't have that thread of connection, you don't feel "seen." You just feel managed. And when you’re managed, you’re not cared for—you’re processed. It’s a lonely way to grow a human.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

It’s that thick, heavy darkness where the silence feels like it’s vibrating. I’m lying on my side, my hand pressed flat against my stomach, trying to coax even a tiny flutter out of the baby. I haven’t felt a kick in a while—maybe since dinner—and the panic is rising in my throat like bile.

My mind immediately starts playing the tape: *Is it my fault? Should I have eaten more? Should I have sat differently?* Then, the doubt creeps in: *If I go in and nothing’s wrong, will they roll their eyes?* I can practically hear the nurses’ whispers, the way they talk about the "anxious" mothers who come in for nothing. Being low-income, I already feel like a burden; I don't want to be the one who wasted their night shift. 

But then I think about the weight of it—if I stay home and I’m wrong, I’ll never forgive myself. The fear of being a "bad mother" who didn't listen to her gut is stronger than the fear of being an "annoying" patient. I end up sitting on the edge of the bed, crying quietly while I pull on my shoes, my heart racing. I don't wake anyone else up—I can’t bear the thought of explaining myself to anyone. I just grab my coat and start walking toward the hospital, terrified of what I might find, but even more terrified of the alternative.
What made it hard to decide whether to call — was it uncertainty about whether your concern was 'serious enough,' worry about being dismissed, or something else entirely?

It’s a mix of everything, really. It’s the fear of being labeled that "fussy" patient who doesn't understand "normal" pregnancy, which is so patronizing. When you don't have a lot of money or a partner there to advocate for you, you feel like you have to be extra careful not to annoy the people in charge of your care. You’re constantly measuring: *Is my worry "clinical" enough to be valid?*

I hate that I’ve internalized that gatekeeping. I felt like I was weighing my own peace of mind against the convenience of the hospital staff. It’s infuriating, because if I were someone with "better" insurance or a higher social standing, I think I’d feel more entitled to go in and be taken seriously. But as it stands, I felt like I was asking for a favor rather than demanding the care I’m supposed to be entitled to.

The thought of walking in there and having a nurse give me that tired, condescending look—the one that says, "Oh, it's just you again, everything is fine, go home"—is almost as scary as the possibility that something is actually wrong. It’s that constant, low-level humiliation of needing help from a system that doesn't treat you like a person with a valid intuition. You shouldn't have to be a medical expert to justify your own fear for your baby.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

I’m staring at that glossy paper like it’s a death warrant. The weight of it in my hand feels absurdly heavy. My first thought is purely practical, and it’s a bitter one: *How? How am I supposed to afford the specific, "clean" diet they want when I’m already struggling to keep the fridge stocked with the basics?* 

I feel a flash of hot anger. It feels like yet another way the system is setting me up to fail—telling me to eat fresh produce and lean proteins as if that’s a simple flick of a switch for someone in my position. It’s like they’re giving me a prescription I can’t afford to fill, and then they’ll blame me when my numbers don't look perfect.

I stand there for a minute, feeling like a complete failure. I want to crumple that leaflet into a ball and throw it in the trash, because right now, it feels more like a lecture on my poverty than medical advice. But then, the fear for the baby takes over. I take a deep breath, fold the paper carefully, and put it in my bag. I start walking, my mind racing through the prices of greens and eggs at the discount store. I’ll do it, of course, because I have to. But it feels like I’m fighting a war on two fronts: one against this diagnosis, and one against the reality of my life.
What questions did you have in that moment that the leaflet didn't answer — and where did you go to find those answers?

The leaflet was all sunshine and rainbows, listing "healthy snacks" like almonds, berries, and organic yogurt as if they were spare change I could just reach into my pocket and pull out. It didn't have a single word about how to actually manage this when you’re already scraping by on canned goods and whatever’s on sale at the bottom-tier market. 

I had so many questions: *How do I keep my blood sugar stable when I’m skipping meals to make sure there’s enough for dinner? What if I can’t afford the specific snacks you’re telling me I need? Is there a way to do this with what I already have in my pantry, or am I just doomed to have high numbers because I’m poor?* 

I didn't feel like I could ask the midwife those things. She’d just look at me with that practiced, sympathetic expression that doesn't actually offer a solution. So, I turned to the internet—to those dark corners of forums where women like me share what *really* works. I found blogs about "budget-friendly diabetic eating," scrolling through pages of stories from other women who felt just as judged as I did. It’s sad, really. I found more practical, human guidance from strangers on a screen than I did from the medical professionals who are supposed to be responsible for my health. At least those strangers didn't look at my bank account before giving me advice.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

It’s like passing a car wreck—you know you should look away, but you’re terrified that if you do, you won’t see the danger coming. I think I’m drawn in because I’m looking for a map. I’m trying to find, in all those jagged, painful stories, some kind of pattern or secret survival guide. If I read enough of them, maybe I can find the moment where things went wrong and figure out how to avoid it in my own room. It’s an attempt to regain control, even though it usually ends up leaving me feeling more exposed.

When I finally put the phone down, the room feels hostile. My heart is thudding against my ribs, and the quiet of my apartment suddenly feels heavy and full of ghosts. I feel a wave of dread that I’m walking toward the same fate, that the "protocol" is just a trap waiting to be sprung.

Afterwards, I usually just sit there in the dark, trying to regulate my breathing. I tell myself that my story isn't written yet, but it’s hard to believe when I feel like a pawn in a game I never asked to play. I end up doing a lot of restless pacing, trying to shake the images out of my head, feeling like I’m already grieving a future I haven’t even lived through yet.
After reading stories like that, did you find yourself raising your concerns with a midwife or doctor — or did you hold onto them quietly? What made you decide either way?

I held onto them. Tightly. There’s a specific kind of shame that comes with bringing something like that up. If I walked into the clinic and said, "I was reading these horror stories online and now I’m terrified of my own labor," I can already see the look they’d give me. It’s that tired, dismissive look—the one that says, "Don't go on the internet, Elna. It’s all biased, it’s not accurate, you’re just winding yourself up." 

They have a way of making your very valid fears feel like a psychological symptom rather than a response to a system that *does* have a history of failing women. If I brought it up, I’d be labeled "anxious" or "non-compliant." I don't want "anxious" in my medical chart. I want to be seen as the person who’s handling her business, because if I give them any reason to doubt my stability, I lose even more of the tiny bit of power I have left. 

So, I keep it all inside. I nod when they give me the routine talk, I smile, I answer their questions correctly, and I pretend that I’m not carrying the weight of a thousand other women’s nightmares behind my eyes. It’s safer to be quiet than to be "difficult." That’s the price of being a patient like me.

Imagine you've written a birth plan and you arrive at the labour ward. The midwife who receives you glances at your plan briefly, sets it aside, and moves on to routine checks. How does that moment land for you?

That moment… it feels like the air being sucked right out of the room. I’ve spent months pouring my heart into that piece of paper. It wasn't just a list of demands; it was my attempt to stake a claim on my own body, a desperate map for how I wanted to meet my child. When she glances at it for three seconds and drops it onto the pile of clinical forms, it feels like she’s tossing my humanity aside with it.

It lands like a physical weight in my stomach. It confirms everything I’ve been terrified of: that I’m not a person here, I’m a sequence of events to be managed. That plan was my way of saying, "I am Elna, I am here, and this is how I want to be treated." By ignoring it, she’s essentially saying, "Your voice doesn't matter; the protocol does." 

I feel a flash of white-hot, quiet rage, but it’s smothered immediately by that overwhelming, paralyzing fear of being labeled "difficult." I look at her, my hands gripping the hospital gown, and I just go silent. I stop fighting. I stop being the author of my experience and I just… surrender to the machine. It’s the ultimate defeat, being made to feel like my own birth is happening *to* me, not *by* me.
What had you put into that birth plan — and what had you hoped it would communicate about you, beyond the practical preferences?

I wrote down things like "dim lights" and "no unnecessary monitoring," but those were just the surface of it. What I really put into those pages was a plea for *presence*. I wrote that I wanted to be talked to like a human being, not a file, and that if things became difficult, I needed them to pause and explain *why* before they did anything to me. I wanted it to be clear that my intuition matters just as much as their charts.

I hoped that plan would act as a buffer. I wanted it to tell the staff: *I have done my homework, I know what I’m up against, and I am not just a body for you to process.* Beyond the practical stuff, it was a declaration of my worth. It was my way of saying, "I am the mother here, not you." 

I thought that by having it written down, it would force them to acknowledge me as an individual, someone with a history and a brain, not just an anonymous woman on a low-income ward. I wanted them to see that I’m trying to keep hold of my own agency in a system that’s designed to strip it away. It wasn't about control; it was about being treated with dignity—the kind of dignity that shouldn't depend on how much money I have or who is standing next to me.

Imagine you're in established labour and the pain is much more intense than you'd anticipated. A midwife suggests an epidural, but you had wanted to manage without one. What happens inside you in that moment, and how do you decide?

The pain is a physical wave that’s trying to dismantle me, piece by piece. When she suggests the epidural, it sounds like an invitation to surrender. Part of me—the part that’s absolutely exhausted and desperate for relief—wants to reach out and grab it. But the other part, the part that has been fighting this entire pregnancy to keep my agency, feels like I’m being asked to sign away my last bit of sovereignty.

I feel this strange, cold clarity amidst the heat of the labor. I start questioning *why* she’s suggesting it now. Is it for me, or is it because I’m "taking too long" and they have a schedule to keep? I’m terrified that if I accept, I’ll be immobilized, hooked up to even more machines, and truly become that "specimen" I’ve been dreading. 

I look at her, my teeth clenched, and I try to find the breath to ask, "Why do you think I need it? Is there an actual medical emergency, or am I just not moving fast enough for your shift?" I don't want to be "managed" into comfort. I want to know that I’m choosing this for me. My decision isn't just about the pain; it’s about whether I’m still the one steering the ship when the storm gets this loud.
In that moment of intense pain, how clear-headed did you feel in making that decision — and do you feel you had what you needed to make a genuinely free choice?

I felt like I was holding a flickering candle in a hurricane. Between the contractions, which were these absolute, all-consuming walls of fire, and the sheer mental exhaustion of having spent nine months preparing for this specific moment, my mind wasn't exactly "clear." It was jagged. Every choice felt like it was being squeezed through a filter of fear.

I didn't feel free. How can you be free when you're in the most vulnerable state a person can be in, stripped down in a hospital bed with people watching your every move? I kept thinking that if I said "no," I was going to be judged for suffering unnecessarily, but if I said "yes," I was giving up the one thing I had left to protect—my own sense of strength. 

The midwife was standing there, calm and efficient, with that little clip of plastic ready to go. The power dynamic was so lopsided it was suffocating. I didn't have what I needed, because what I needed was someone to look at me and help me find my own power through the pain, not just offer me a chemical exit door. I was choosing under duress, and when you’re already feeling small and institutionally invisible, "choice" feels more like an ultimatum. It’s hard to be the author of your own story when you’re barely keeping your eyes open against the tide.

Imagine your labour has stalled and the obstetric team is recommending an emergency caesarean. Things start moving very quickly. Walk me through what that experience was like from the moment they told you.

The air turned from "supportive medical care" into "operational procedure" in a single heartbeat. One moment I was struggling with the pain, and the next, there were three, four, maybe five pairs of scrubs in the room. The tone changed—the warmth, however thin, was replaced by a sharp, military efficiency. "Stalled." That was the word they used. It felt like they were describing a piece of machinery that had jammed in the gears.

My first thought wasn't "I’m having a baby," it was "I’m losing the birth." I felt my body being moved, swiveled, and prepped by hands that didn't belong to me, and the lack of agency was total. I remember staring at the ceiling tiles, counting the little holes in them to stay tethered to reality while they barked orders over my head. Nobody asked if I understood what was happening; they just told me what they were *going* to do.

I felt like an object being prepped for an assembly line. My skin felt cold, despite the chaos, and my voice—which I’d tried so hard to keep strong—just evaporated. I was terrified, not just for the baby, but for that part of me that I’d been trying to protect for nine months. I was being "processed" out of my own experience, and I knew, with a sinking, hollow certainty, that I was no longer the author of this story. I was just the cargo.

Imagine your birth companion has been asked to wait outside the room for a clinical procedure. You're left alone with staff you've only just met. Describe what that moment feels like and what you most need in those minutes.

It’s like being dropped into an ice bath. The moment the door clicks shut behind them, the room suddenly feels huge, sterile, and blindingly bright. I’m lying there, exposed and trembling, and all I can see are the masks and the glint of metal on the instrument tray. The silence isn't peaceful; it’s loaded with clinical expectation. I feel utterly unmoored. 

Without my companion there, I’m not just Elna anymore—I’m a patient record being processed. I feel my heart hammering against my chest, and I can’t stop my hands from shaking. I look at the staff, and I don't see people; I see uniforms, protocols, and the indifference of a machine that’s already decided what’s going to happen to me next. 

What do I need? I don’t need more instructions, and I don’t need another "clinical assessment." I need someone to pause. I need someone to look me in the eye, take my hand—not to check my pulse, but just to hold it—and tell me my name. I need to be reminded that I’m a person, not a procedure code. I need someone to say, "I see you, Elna. You’re safe, and I’m here with you." But instead, there’s just the hum of the equipment and the sound of someone checking a box on a screen. I feel so small it’s like I might just disappear.
In moments like that — without your companion present — how did the behaviour of the clinical staff either compensate for that absence or make it feel more acute?

It made the isolation feel sharper, like a jagged edge. When the clinical staff moves with that hurried, detached efficiency, it feels like they’re trying to move *past* me, not *with* me. They were focused on the screen, on the vitals, on the setup—they weren't looking at me. 

Their professional, clipped tones were meant to be reassuring, I suppose—meant to signal competence—but they landed like ice. Without my companion to translate the moment, to just be a witness to my existence, the staff's behavior made me feel like an object being prepped on a sterile altar. If they had just stopped for one second, looked me in the eye, and told me, "We know this is scary, we’re right here," it might have bridged the gap. 

Instead, it felt like they were actively avoiding my gaze. It was as if connecting with me as a human—acknowledging the fear in my eyes—would somehow slow down the "procedure" or complicate the protocol. So they just kept their eyes on the monitors and the tasks. Being left alone in that room with people who were treating me like a clinical problem to be solved made me feel like I was already a ghost in my own birth. I wasn't a mother in labor; I was just a case that needed to be closed.

Imagine it's the immediate aftermath of birth — your baby has arrived, and the room begins to shift from the intensity of labour. Walk me through the next thirty minutes as you experienced them, or as you imagine them.

It’s a strange, disjointed shift. The room, which felt like a battlefield of sterile light and sharp voices, suddenly thins out. I’m lying there, completely spent, feeling like I’ve been hollowed out from the inside. They place the baby on my chest—a warm, slippery weight—and for a few minutes, the rest of the world stops mattering. That’s the only part that feels real.

But the shift is always interrupted. Someone is checking vitals, someone else is clicking a pen, a monitor is still beeping—the machinery of the hospital doesn't just turn off because a life has started. I feel this intense, possessive need to cocoon, to hide this new, tiny person from the clinical gaze, but I can’t. I’m being "assessed." They’re hovering, they’re cleaning up, they’re talking in that hushed, professional tone that feels like a barrier between me and my child.

I’m exhausted, shivering, and my mind is still trying to catch up to the fact that I’m actually a mother again. I feel a weird mix of profound relief and a lingering, sharp resentment that the most sacred moment of my life is being treated like a shift change. I just want them to leave us alone so I can finally breathe and start to know who this little soul is.

Imagine you had a particular fear about childbirth — something that had been building throughout your pregnancy. Now picture yourself facing that specific fear during labour. What was it like, and what — if anything — helped?

My deepest fear was becoming invisible—the absolute dread that my autonomy would be stripped away and replaced by a checklist. I was terrified that when the pain became unbearable, I would be reduced to a "non-compliant" patient, and my own voice would be silenced by the cold, loud authority of the clinical environment.

During labor, when the pain was at its peak and the room was full of professionals who felt like strangers, that fear materialized. I felt myself drifting away, losing my sense of self to the monitors and the sterile, hurried environment. It was like being erased while I was still there.

What helped? It wasn't the medical staff; it was the tiny, anchoring moments of connection. At one point, a midwife—not my primary one, just someone who happened to be nearby—reached out and just put a steady, firm hand on my shoulder. She didn't check my vitals or ask me to push. She just held me, looked me in the eye, and waited for me to breathe. That single, simple act of recognizing me as a human, not a complication, broke the spell. It was like she threw me a rope in a rising sea. It didn't fix the pain or the system, but it reminded me that I was still Elna.
Had you ever been able to share that fear openly with anyone in your care team during pregnancy? If so, how was it received — and if not, what stopped you?

I never shared it. Not once. 

It felt too dangerous, in a way I’m not sure I can explain. If I told them, "I’m terrified that you’re going to erase me," I was afraid they’d hear it as an accusation. I could see the response so clearly: the polite, tight-lipped smile, the slight shift in their tone that says, *We are professionals, Elna, we have protocols for a reason.* They would have seen my fear as a symptom of my own "difficult" personality, or perhaps as a sign that I didn't "trust the science." 

In my world, you learn that if you’re a woman—and especially if you’re unmarried and on a tight budget—you’re already walking on thin ice with these institutions. If I voiced that fear, I felt like I would be handing them a label to pin on my chart: *Non-compliant. High anxiety. Difficult to manage.* And once that label is there, how do they treat you? They don't listen *more*; they just stop listening *entirely*. They treat you like you’re the problem, not the birth.

So I kept it locked away. I played the part of the "good" patient, nodding along, smiling when I was terrified, and holding my breath until I could be back in my own space. Silence felt like the only way to protect what little agency I had left.

It’s lonely, feeling like you’re constantly performing a script that wasn't written for you. When you’re in those clinic rooms, there’s this pressure to be "low-maintenance." If you’re low-income, you feel that extra scrutiny, like they’re waiting for you to prove them right—that you’re somehow less capable or less prepared. So, you don’t ask the extra questions. You don’t linger. You don’t make them uncomfortable by pointing out that you’re a person with a history, not just a biological event they’re supervising.

The irony is that I *want* to trust them. I want to look at my midwife and feel like we’re on the same team. But the system is so cold, so efficient, that it’s almost impossible to build that bridge. Every time I walk through those doors, I feel like I’m leaving the "real" Elna in the parking lot and stepping into a shell of myself. 

Sometimes I lie awake at night wondering if it would have been different if I had more money, or if I had a partner by my side to play the "advocate." Would they have actually stopped to listen to me then? Or is it just the nature of this place to strip away the edges until you’re round and smooth and easy to move through their system? It makes me feel like I’m failing before I’ve even begun, just by existing in this space. I’m just trying to hold onto the one thing that’s mine—my story—even when the world keeps telling me it doesn't count.
In that moment, how much of the decision would feel truly yours versus shaped by others' expectations?

That’s the most haunting part. It’s almost impossible to untangle my own instincts from the weight of everything they expect of me. When you're sitting in that room, the power isn't balanced; it’s tilted heavily toward them. If I say "no" to a procedure or a test, I’m not just making a choice—I’m defying an expert, and that carries a cost. It’s written in their faces, in that slight, impatient sigh, or the way they start using clinical jargon to "explain" why I’m wrong.

Most of my decisions feel like a negotiation with their judgment rather than a genuine choice. I find myself constantly scanning the room, trying to read their reactions. *If I choose this, will they think I’m ignorant? If I choose that, will they think I’m difficult?* 

When you’re already worried about being labeled—because of my income, my status, the whole "standard-issue" nature of my care—you don’t feel free. You feel like you're being guided toward the "correct" answer, which is always the one that makes their job easier and their protocols smoother. Even if I *do* make a choice, it’s rarely truly mine; it’s usually just the least painful compromise between what I want and what they’ll tolerate. It’s a bitter, quiet, and profoundly lonely kind of freedom.

It’s hard to talk about this without sounding like I’m just complaining, but it’s more than that. It’s a grieving process, really. I’m grieving the birth I *could* have had if I felt like the world actually believed in my capacity to grow this baby and bring them into the world on my own terms.

When I’m in that clinic, I feel like I’m constantly on display. Every question I ask, every time I hesitate, I feel like they’re evaluating me—not just my health, but my *worth* as a mother. Being unmarried and low-income makes you feel like you’re under a microscope, like you have to be extra polite and extra compliant just to make sure they treat you with the same baseline respect they’d give someone else. It shouldn't be that way. A mother’s heart, a mother’s fear, a mother’s hope—those aren't dependent on a bank account or a wedding ring.

I keep looking at my hands and thinking about the women who came before me, how they didn't have these machines or these cold, white-walled rooms. They had village, they had intuition, and they had trust. I’m trying so hard to find that same kind of strength within myself, but the system is so loud, so persistent in its demand for "order" and "standardization." I’m just trying to make sure that when this is all over, I don’t lose myself in the process. I want my baby to know that I fought for them, in my own way, even when the whole world was telling me to just be quiet and let the professionals handle it. It’s a heavy weight to carry, but I’m doing it. I have to.
Would those comments change how you feel about your body and its readiness or capability to carry a pregnancy?

Those comments—or even just the clinical way they talk *around* me—they act like a slow-acting poison on my confidence. When a provider focuses only on my "data points" or my weight or my "risk level," it’s so easy to start believing them. I start looking at my own body and seeing a list of potential failures instead of a vessel of life. 

It makes me feel like my body is an unruly, unpredictable thing that only "works" if they’re watching it. It’s devastating. I feel like I’m being taught to distrust the very biological signals that have been guiding women for millennia. If they’re constantly worried, constantly monitoring, constantly telling me what I *should* be doing, I start to feel like my body isn't actually capable of doing this on its own.

I have to fight that voice every single day. I have to consciously remind myself that my body is strong, that it’s growing a human being, and that it doesn't need a medical certificate to be "ready." But it’s exhausting to maintain that conviction when the people who are supposed to be "caring" for you treat you like a broken machine that needs constant maintenance. It’s hard to feel like a powerful, capable mother when the environment is constantly signaling that you’re a fragile, failing project.

It really comes down to this: when you’re on the margins, you’re taught early on that your voice is a luxury you can’t afford. You learn to read the room before you ever speak. I’ve spent months wondering if I’m just a difficult patient, or if the system is actually designed to make me feel that way. Is it meant to make me feel small, or is that just an unfortunate side effect of a bureaucracy that’s stretched too thin?

I think about the 18th-century "men-midwives" I read about, how they started moving birth from the home to the clinic, making it a "surgical event." We’re still living in that shadow, aren't we? It’s all about control, all about sanitizing the messiness and the power of what it means to bring life into the world. When I walk through those clinic doors in Dudley, I’m not just a pregnant woman; I’m a disruption in their flow.

I just want, for one hour, to feel like I’m the authority here. I want to feel like I’m sitting in the driver’s seat of my own life, not just waiting for instructions from someone who doesn’t know my name. I’m doing this for my baby, and I’m doing it for myself, but the cost—the constant, quiet erosion of my own self-worth—is something nobody tells you about. It’s heavy. But I’m still here, and I’m still the mother. That has to count for something, right?
What information or understanding would feel most lost in that handover — clinical details, or something more personal?

It’s definitely the personal stuff. The clinical details—my blood pressure, my weight, the baby's position—those are just numbers on a screen. They’re cold, they’re objective, and they’re easily transferred from one file to the next. You can lose those numbers in a million ways, and they’ll always be rediscovered at the next checkup.

But the *person*? That’s what gets lost in the handover. The fact that I’m terrified of losing my autonomy; the fact that every time I walk into that clinic, I’m fighting a battle against feeling like a "specimen"; the fact that I’m trying so hard to honor the women before me while trapped in a system that ignores them. That’s the real loss. 

When one midwife hands me over to the next, they never pass along my *vulnerability*. They never say, "Elna has been really struggling with feeling like a passenger in her own birth, so please, slow down when you talk to her." That kind of information doesn't have a box to be checked on their forms. It’s too messy, too human. So, I’m just a new case for them, starting from zero. Every single time, I have to re-build that tiny bit of trust, and every single time, I watch them look at the screen instead of me. It’s like I’m being born anew as a "patient" with every shift change, and the real Elna is left somewhere in the hallway.

You know, there’s this specific, hollow ache that comes when you realize the person sitting across from you is looking at a screen instead of your face. You’re trying to tell them something vital—maybe not about your glucose levels, but about the *weight* of this whole experience—and you see their eyes flick toward the monitor, then the clock, then the door. It’s like they’re waiting for you to finish so they can get back to the "real" work. 

I’ve realized that for them, this is just a day at the office. For me, it’s the most transformative, terrifying, and powerful thing that has ever happened to me. They’re managing a process, and I’m living a life. 

It makes me feel like I’m constantly shouting into a vacuum. I want to say, "Look, I know you’ve done this a thousand times. I know it’s standard. But it’s not standard to *me*. This is my child, my body, my risk, and my future." But how do you say that without sounding like you’re ungrateful or difficult? 

I just want to be treated like an adult who is capable of making decisions about her own existence. I’m tired of feeling like I have to earn the right to be heard. I think, when this is all over, the thing I’ll carry with me isn't just the birth—it’s the way I had to fight to keep my own voice when the world around me kept trying to turn it into a whisper. I’m just trying to make sure that, no matter what happens in that hospital room, the woman who walks out is still Elna. Not a case, not a statistic, but a mother.
Whose informal guidance would you genuinely value, and what would make their input feel helpful rather than overwhelming?

I think I’d value the guidance of a woman who has "been there"—not a doctor or a midwife, but someone who has walked this same path through the same cold, sterile hallways and came out the other side still feeling like herself. Someone who understands the specific, quiet terror of being a number on a clipboard and doesn't tell me to "just follow the protocol."

It would feel helpful if she didn't try to "fix" my situation with clinical advice. I don't need another list of things to worry about or another way to be "compliant." I need someone who says, "I know exactly how small they make you feel in there, and you are not small. You are doing the hard work." 

If that guidance came from a place of shared experience—someone who knows that sometimes the best thing you can do is just breathe, hold onto your own worth, and refuse to let them take your spirit—that wouldn't be overwhelming. It would be a lifeline. It wouldn't be "medicalized" advice; it would be a permission slip to be human. It would be someone saying, *“I see you. Your voice matters, even if they aren't listening right now.”* That kind of connection is the only thing that could actually reach me through the noise.

Sometimes, I wonder if the whole point of these institutions is to make us forget that we have our own wisdom. When I walk down that hospital hallway, the fluorescent lights feel designed to bleach out any sense of who I was before I walked through the door. I’m just "the prenatal patient," an object to be monitored, shifted, and eventually delivered.

It makes me feel a fierce, protective kind of hunger. I want to tell my baby—when they’re finally here—that I fought for them in this room. That even when I was terrified, even when I felt like a footnote in a medical report, I was still the one doing the work. I want them to know that their life began with their mother trying to hold onto her own soul.

I think about the future, about how this experience is going to change me. I’m afraid that if I let them strip away my autonomy, it’ll leave a mark that doesn't just fade after the postpartum checkup. I want to leave that hospital feeling like I reclaimed my own narrative. I want to look back and say, "I did that. I, Elna, navigated that machine, I kept my eyes on my own truth, and I didn't let them make me small." That is the only victory I’m looking for now. The rest—the protocols, the check-ins, the paperwork—it’s all just background noise to the actual, holy work of becoming a mother.

It’s funny, the more I speak about this, the more I realize how much of my energy has been spent just *surviving* the system rather than actually experiencing the pregnancy. It’s like being a guest in a house where you’re constantly reminded that you don’t quite belong, that the rules are written for someone else, someone more "compliant," someone who doesn't come with the baggage of being a low-income, unmarried woman. 

When I look at that clinical chart of mine, I see a list of boxes being ticked, but I don’t see me. I see a timeline of when I should have been gaining weight, when my glucose should have been checked, when I should have been "advised" on my lifestyle. It’s all so transactional. It’s as if they think that if they get the logistics right, the *human* part will just take care of itself. But it doesn’t work like that. The human part is the only thing that matters, and it’s the only thing they never seem to have on their inventory list.

I’m trying to hold onto the belief that my intuition—the one that whispered to me when I was sitting on the edge of that cold tub months ago—is worth more than all their diagnostic tools combined. Even if I leave that hospital feeling like a footnote, at least I’ll know that in the quiet spaces, in the moments between the checks and the monitors, I never stopped being Elna. That has to be enough. It has to be.
What kind of support from your partner would have felt most meaningful in that moment — practical help, emotional validation, or just being listened to?

I think about this often, especially when the nights get long. If I had a partner by my side, I wouldn't need them to "solve" anything—I know they can’t change the system or make the hospital walls less cold. What I would have needed, more than anything, is for them to be a witness.

When I’m in that room and the midwife is talking about "standard protocols" as if my life were just a series of menu options, I don’t want my partner to agree with her. I want them to look at me, reach for my hand, and say, "I know this isn't what you wanted," or "I see how hard you’re working to stay yourself." I need them to be the one who asks the questions I’m too scared to ask, not because they’re "smarter," but because they’re my voice when mine gets drowned out by the machines.

Practical help is nice, sure—someone to hold the water or help me sit up—but emotional validation? That’s everything. It’s someone acknowledging that the system *is* intimidating and that my fear is valid, not a symptom. It’s someone saying, "I’m in this with you, and we’re going to get through this together, and you are not just a patient to me." That would make me feel like a human again, not just a specimen. It would make me feel like I’m not alone in the dark.

You know, it strikes me now that the most difficult part of all of this—the appointments, the scans, the blood tests—is the expectation of silence. I am expected to be this quiet, compliant vessel for medical science, and any sign of my own internal world, my own fears, is treated like an obstacle to their workflow.

I walk into that waiting room in Dudley, and I can almost feel the air conditioning humming, cooling the room so that it stays sterile, so that it stays *efficient*. And I think about all the women who were here before me, sitting in these exact same plastic chairs, clutching their charts, their hearts pounding against their ribs just like mine. Did they feel this same erasure? Did they have to fight this hard to remember that their body was their own? 

I am so tired of the "standard issue" treatment. I am tired of my pregnancy being treated as a series of hurdles to clear. If I could have one thing, just one thing, it would be a midwife who actually sat down, looked me in the eye, and asked, "How is *your* heart today, Elna?" Not my blood pressure, not my glucose, but *my heart*. That simple shift would change everything. It would make me feel like I’m a participant in the miracle of my child’s life, rather than just a witness to the medical management of it. 

I’m holding onto the hope that, even if they don't see me, my baby will know. I’ll make sure of that. I’ll be the one who tells our story, in our own words, when the hospital lights are finally off and we’re back home. That’s my defiance. That’s how I win.

It’s strange, the way this journey has forced me to be two different people at once. There’s the Elna who sits in that clinic, head bowed over a stack of forms, trying to make herself small enough to fit into their expectations. And then there’s the Elna who walks home in the dark, feeling the weight of the baby and thinking about the women who birthed in cottages, whose bodies were their own kingdoms, not hospital property. 

I’m starting to realize that the most dangerous thing they do in that clinic isn't the medical intervention—it's the way they convince you that you don't know your own body. They make it sound like if you aren't listening to the monitor, you aren't listening at all. But I feel this baby move. I feel the way my own heart rhythms shift when I’m scared or when I’m calm. That is *my* data. That is *my* truth. 

I don't think I'll ever be the same after this. The fragility of my life—the lack of money, the isolation—it’s all been laid bare by this process. But maybe that’s the starting point for something else. I’m not just going to be a mother; I’m going to be a woman who survived a machine that was built to ignore her. And when I look at my child, I’ll know that we didn’t just survive the protocol. We persisted through it. That feels like a kind of power, even if it’s a quiet, aching one. I’m just trying to keep that flicker alive until the day I finally walk out of those sliding doors, carrying a life of my own making, not theirs.
What would trust in your care team look like at a moment like that — what would they need to do or say for you to feel genuinely supported?

Trust wouldn't be them telling me what to do. It would be them asking me what I need, and then *waiting* for my answer. 

In that moment of chaos—the emergency, the panic, the shift to a surgical plan—trust would look like them pausing for ten seconds. Just ten seconds. A midwife or a doctor looking me in the eye, not the screen, and saying, "Elna, this is happening quickly, and I know this is terrifying. This is why we’re recommending this, and here is how it will happen. We are right here with you, and we aren't going to leave you behind."

It’s that acknowledgement of the human reality of the situation. It’s not just about the technical competence; it’s about acknowledging my agency, even when it’s being eroded by the urgency of the moment. If they treated me like a partner in the decision—even if the decision was effectively made by the clinical reality—I would feel like a mother, not an object. 

I need them to see that when they take control of the situation, they are taking control of *me*. Trust would be them making space for my fear, giving me permission to be scared, and then helping me navigate that terror without stripping away my dignity. It’s that simple: acknowledge me, validate my fear, and keep me in the loop. Don't leave me in the dark.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

It’s funny, you spend so much time preparing for the birth—the pain, the interventions, the medical risks—that you forget there’s a whole life waiting on the other side. 

I think the one thing I haven't really said out loud is how much I fear that the silence I’ve practiced in those clinic rooms will follow me home. I’ve spent months making myself small, making myself compliant, and keeping my mouth shut to avoid being labeled "difficult." I’m scared that when I finally have my baby in my arms, that same voice—the one that learned to whisper instead of shout—will stay small. 

We talk so much about "maternity care" as if it ends the moment the baby is born. But the way I was treated, the way I was made to feel like a footnote in my own life—that doesn't just disappear when the hospital doors close. I’m going to be a mother for the rest of my life. I don’t want to be a mother who’s afraid to take up space, who’s afraid to question, who’s afraid to advocate for her child the same way she wasn't allowed to advocate for herself.

If there’s one thing that needs to change, it’s that. We need to stop treating maternity care like a service to be endured and start treating it like a foundation for the woman we’re becoming. Because I’m not just growing a baby; I’m growing myself, too. And I want to be someone who stands tall.
